verses.text: 'How long will ye vex my soul, and break me in pieces with words?' Job asks; if he has erred, the fault rests with himself. He pleads against their relentless blows. Even if a sufferer has faults, ten reproaches do not heal a single wound.

'How long will ye vex my soul, and break me in pieces with words?' Job asks; if he has erred, the fault rests with himself. He pleads against their relentless blows. Even if a sufferer has faults, ten reproaches do not heal a single wound.

They magnify themselves against him and use his disgrace as proof; he cries out of wrong but is not heard. He feels condemned without a hearing. To the afflicted, the silence of justice is itself a fresh affliction.

They magnify themselves against him and use his disgrace as proof; he cries out of wrong but is not heard. He feels condemned without a hearing. To the afflicted, the silence of justice is itself a fresh affliction.

They magnify themselves against him and use his disgrace as proof; he cries out of wrong but is not heard. He feels condemned without a hearing. To the afflicted, the silence of justice is itself a fresh affliction.

Job feels God has fenced his way, stripped his honour, uprooted his hope, and counts him an enemy, His troops encamped around him. He reads every loss as God's hostility. In the darkness, even a saint may mistake the Father's discipline for a foe's assault.

God, he feels, has driven his brethren far off; his kinsfolk fail him, and his servants ignore his call. He is left utterly alone. Isolation is among the bitterest parts of suffering, when even the nearest withdraw.

God, he feels, has driven his brethren far off; his kinsfolk fail him, and his servants ignore his call. He is left utterly alone. Isolation is among the bitterest parts of suffering, when even the nearest withdraw.

His breath is strange to his wife, children despise him, and his closest friends abhor him. The man once honoured is now loathed by all. There are valleys where the believer tastes the loneliness that Christ Himself would fully drink.

Wasted to skin and bone, he begs, 'have pity upon me... O ye my friends; for the hand of God hath touched me.' He pleads for a little mercy. When God's hand lies heavy on a man, his friends should pity, not pile on more pain.

Job wishes his words were written in a book, engraved in rock forever. He senses he is about to say something worth keeping. The Spirit was preparing the great confession that would outlast the ages.
Job wishes his words were written in a book, engraved in rock forever. He senses he is about to say something worth keeping. The Spirit was preparing the great confession that would outlast the ages.

Then faith blazes out: 'I know that my redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth,' and in his own flesh he shall see God. From the ash-heap Job sees a living Redeemer and a resurrection. This is the gospel hope shining in the oldest of books.

He warns the friends to fear the sword themselves, 'that ye may know there is a judgment.' He turns their threats around. All who wrongly condemn the righteous must remember they too will stand before the Judge.
